Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Right Fast, averaging 134.4 kph (up to 141.5).
  • Typical length is 7-8m (their good length); median 7.3 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length on the stumps, swinging away, seaming both ways, passing outside off (20% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 37% of the time against left-handers.
  • Round the wicket he shifts his pitching line from stumps to wide of 6th.
  • Across an over he holds a fairly consistent length.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Full (4 wkts, 2.0% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: 5th stump.
  • Takes 19% of wickets pitching a good length on the stumps.
  • Beats the bat 6.1% of tracked balls (false-shot 16.6%).
  • Gets you bowled more than most — 24% of his wickets (1.3× the typical rate for this type).
  • 50% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, midwicket: regulation, square leg: deep.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length down the leg side (13% of runs).
  • Short ball: 35% of deliveries, economy 4.0 — 7 wkts from 381 short balls.
  • Away from full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (44%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes back of a length down the leg side (economy 4.2, beats the bat only 18%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs left-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length on the stumps, swinging away, seaming both ways, passing outside off (20% of deliveries, economy 1.94). Minor variations: a good length down the leg side (13%) and a good length outside off (13%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length on the stumps ▶swinging away, seaming both ways20%1.94318%
a good length down the leg side ▶swinging both ways, seaming both ways13%3.66120%
a good length outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways13%2.23219%
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways11%2.49217%
back of a length down the leg side ▶seaming both ways, swinging both ways8%4.21118%
full outside off ▶swinging away, seaming back7%5.91322%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs left-handers)

57% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 12 balls; most runs go to the leg side (44%); the drive hurts most (37 fours/sixes at 2.6 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at wide of 6th — pitches around stumps and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the off side (51%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length on the stumps most often (20%); drops short often (35%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length on the stumps, but strikes most often pitching on the 5th stump.

Speed & Spells

He drops ~2.3 km/h from his opening spell to later spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.
Speed by Spell
By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Round the wicket his pitching line moves from stumps to wide of 6th — economy 3.61→3.27, a wicket every 85→44 balls (over→round).

Sequencing & Over Construction

Moderately consistent with his length (length spread ±2.0 m; more repeatable than 46% of pace bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Doubles up on the short ball — pitches up only 28% of the time after banging one in; and tends to strike with a straighter ball after working across the batter (31% straighter than the ball before).
Across 38 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.

Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A mid-height release point; releases about 68 cm from the middle stump; varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 98% of right-arm pace).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
